docs.title: Sanctions and Export Controls — AI, Advanced Computing & Oil/Energy
docs.text:
A curated resource on US export controls and sanctions as applied to artificial intelligence, advanced computing, and the oil/energy sector.

Maintained by Felipe Villasuso Lago (AIQ / London South Bank University — LSBU).

Disclaimer: This resource is for informational and academic purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. All content is compiled exclusively from publicly available sources — official government publications, regulatory texts, legislative materials, press releases, and open-access analysis. No proprietary, confidential, subscription-only, or privileged material is reproduced. Entries in the designations register that could not be retrieved at the time of indexing are flagged accordingly and based solely on publicly stated metadata. Always consult qualified export-control and sanctions counsel before taking any compliance decision.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This repository collects primary regulatory sources, law-firm analysis, enforcement guidance, and weekly news digests covering:

  • Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) Export Administration Regulations (EAR)
  • OFAC sanctions programmes and the 50% / UBO aggregation rule
  • AI Diffusion Rule and advanced-computing export controls
  • BIS Affiliates Rule (snaps back 10 November 2026)
  • Encryption controls and open-source software scope
  • Extraterritorial jurisdiction and enforcement
  • Sanctions exposure in the oil, gas & energy sector

docs.title: Related Repositories
docs.text:
Part of a connected research and monitoring stack on AI governance, export controls, and joint-venture / energy-transition governance:

  • AI Regulatory Monitor — automated tracking of AI, export-control, and sanctions rulemaking (Federal Register triage loop, Anthropic API, Slack alerting).
  • Sanctions & Export Controls Monitor — daily monitoring of BIS, OFAC, Entity List / MEU List / SDN developments, with a standing focus on the BIS Affiliates Rule (50% Rule) and its 10 Nov 2026 reimposition.
  • Responsible-AI-evaluation — evaluation methods and governance frameworks for responsible AI deployment.
  • Joint Ventures and Energy Trilemma — comparative JV governance research (UAE / Norway), CCUS source registers, and the "computational sovereignty" thesis linking compute infrastructure to national energy autonomy.

Built on MkDocs Material with GitHub Actions for continuous, auditable updates.

docs.title: OFAC 50% Rule: Ownership Aggregation Chains
docs.text:
The diagram below illustrates how SDN restrictions flow through ownership structures — including cases where no single SDN holds ≥ 50% alone.

graph TD
    SDN1["🔴 SDN Entity A\n(e.g. sanctioned bank)"]
    SDN2["🔴 SDN Entity B\n(e.g. state oil company)"]

    SDN1 -->|"owns 60%"| Sub1["⛔ Subsidiary X\nBLOCKED — 60% ≥ 50%"]
    SDN1 -->|"owns 30%"| JV["Joint Venture Co."]
    SDN2 -->|"owns 25%"| JV
    JV   -->|"30% + 25% = 55%\naggregate ≥ 50%"| JV_BLOCKED["⛔ Joint Venture Co.\nBLOCKED — aggregate rule"]
    Sub1 -->|"owns 80%"| GC["⛔ Grandchild Entity\nBLOCKED — indirect chain"]
    SDN1 -->|"owns 40%"| Safe["✅ Holding Co.\nNOT blocked — 40% < 50%\n(verify no other SDN stake)"]

    style SDN1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style SDN2 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Sub1 fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style JV_BLOCKED fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style GC fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style Safe fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style JV fill:#f97316,color:#fff

Key rule: Aggregation applies even where no single SDN holds ≥ 50% alone. Always trace full ownership chains and add up all SDN-held stakes. See OFAC FAQ 1521.

docs.title: Anthropic Fable 5 / Mythos 5 — BIS Deemed-Export Directive (June 2026)
docs.text:
In June 2026, the US Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) issued a licence-requirement directive targeting Anthropic's two frontier AI models — Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — within days of their commercial launch, compelling a global suspension of user access pending licence authorisation. The action is the first known application of a BIS "is informed" letter to the real-time access interface of a deployed SaaS AI capability, rather than to hardware, source code, or model weights, and it raises foundational questions about the scope of deemed-export controls in cloud-delivered AI environments.

Source and verification status: The BIS directive letter has not been made public. Its existence and basic terms are known through Anthropic's public statement and third-party reporting. The statutory basis, the specific licence triggers, and the BIS factual assessment underlying the directive are established by inference from those sources and from standard EAR deemed-export doctrine. Items assessed as contested or single-sourced are labelled explicitly in the Contested Items section below.

docs.title: Key Facts
docs.text:
• Models affected: Claude Fable 5; Mythos 5 (Anthropic, launched 9 June 2026)
  • Directive type: BIS "is informed" letter — licence requirement
  • Directive received: 12 June 2026, 17:21 ET
  • Response: Global suspension of API and consumer access (not geofenced)
  • Agency: Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), US Department of Commerce
  • Asserted authorities: ECRA 50 U.S.C. § 4817; EAR 15 CFR § 744.22; deemed-export provisions 15 CFR §§ 734.13, 734.14, 734.16
  • Status: Suspension in effect as of indexing; licence application(s) filed; outcome unresolved / contested

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: Date Event 9 Jun 2026 Fable 5 and Mythos 5 launched commercially 12 Jun 2026, 17:21 ET BIS "is informed" letter received by Anthropic 12 Jun 2026 (evening) Anthropic disables global API and consumer access Post-12 Jun 2026 Licence application(s) filed; outcome pending June 2026 (ongoing) Merits dispute publicly joined; litigation posture forming on related Pentagon designation

docs.title: Mechanism: The "Is Informed" Letter
docs.text:
Under the Export Administration Regulations, BIS may notify an exporter by letter that a specific export licence is required for a particular item or transaction — even where the item was previously eligible for a licence exception or no licence was required. Once formally notified ("is informed"), the exporter cannot claim ignorance; continued export without a licence is a wilful violation under the EAR.

The letter reportedly identified four categories of licence trigger under EAR § 744.22 (licence requirements for items controlled under emerging and foundational technology authority, implemented under ECRA § 4817):

  1. Access by nationals of Country Group D:1 or D:5 states — including China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea — regardless of where those nationals are physically located at the time of access.
  2. Deemed export to those nationals' home countries when access occurs within the United States, under 15 CFR §§ 734.13–734.16.
  3. End-use in military, intelligence, or national-security applications.
  4. Use in developing, fine-tuning, or distilling successor or derivative AI models of equivalent or greater capability.

Note: The full text of the directive is unpublished. The four triggers listed above are reconstructed from Anthropic's public statement and practitioner analysis. The precise regulatory language in the letter has not been independently confirmed. [Contested — see Contested Items]

docs.title: Deemed-Export Doctrine Applied to SaaS AI
docs.text:
The deemed-export doctrine (15 CFR §§ 734.13–734.16) provides that a "release" of controlled technology or source code to a foreign national within the United States constitutes an export to that person's home country. Historically, the doctrine has been applied to: physical access to controlled hardware in research facilities; disclosure of technical parameters to foreign national employees; and transfer of software source code.

Applied to a SaaS AI model, BIS's apparent theory is that a foreign national's interactive use of Fable 5 or Mythos 5 — including receipt of model outputs, access to API responses, and the potential for inference-time extraction of model behaviour — constitutes a "release" of the controlled technology to that person and, by deemed export, to their country of nationality. This extends the doctrine to an artefact category — a hosted inference endpoint — that has no settled precedent in EAR enforcement history.

docs.title: Why the Suspension Was Global, Not Geofenced
docs.text:
Geofencing — blocking access from the territory of specific countries — would not satisfy the deemed-export compliance obligation because the trigger is the nationality of the accessing user, not their physical location. A Chinese national accessing Fable 5 from London, Paris, or within the United States would equally constitute a deemed export to China under BIS's theory.

The only compliant alternative to a blanket suspension was to verify the nationality and citizenship of every user in real time before each API call — technically impractical for a production SaaS deployment at scale, and legally uncertain in multiple jurisdictions. The directive therefore compelled a global access suspension regardless of the geographic origin of traffic.

docs.title: Doctrinal Significance: From Technology to Access Control
docs.text:
Prior BIS enforcement and rulemaking targeting AI capabilities has focused on:

  • Chip hardware — ECCN 3A090 and Total Processing Performance (TPP) thresholds applied at the point of physical or documentary export; see AI & Advanced Technology Export Controls.
  • Model weights — ECCN classifications introduced by the January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule and the three-tier country framework.
  • Software and source code — 5D002 and encryption controls; the "publicly available" carve-out under 15 CFR § 734.7.

The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 directive marks the first known application of an "is informed" licence requirement to the real-time access interface of a deployed SaaS AI — the API endpoint and consumer interface — without any assertion that model weights were transferred or source code exported. The controlled item, on BIS's apparent theory, is the inferential output stream itself: what the model produces in response to user queries.

If this theory is sustained, the compliance implications are structural:

  • Any sufficiently capable AI API could in principle be subject to deemed-export licence requirements if BIS determines its capability threshold meets the ECRA § 4817 "emerging or foundational technology" standard, irrespective of what its developer intends to export.
  • The control point shifts from a one-time export event (customs clearance for hardware; deemed release of source code) to continuous access management: an ongoing obligation attached to every API call made by every user of every nationality.
  • AI API providers become, in effect, perpetual exporters of a controlled capability, with compliance status contingent on user identity verification at a granularity the current EAR deemed-export framework was not designed to require.

This structural point connects directly to the computational sovereignty thesis — developed in the Joint Ventures and Energy Trilemma repository — that AI model dependencies, like energy infrastructure dependencies, embed geopolitical exposure that can be activated unilaterally by the regulating state. A BIS "is informed" letter functions as a kill switch: it can be delivered at 17:21 on any evening and take effect immediately, regardless of the commercial relationships or technical architectures it disrupts.

See also BIS Affiliates Rule (parallel expansion of EAR controls through ownership-chain attribution); Extraterritorial Enforcement (FDPR and other mechanisms BIS uses beyond the US border).

docs.title: The Merits Dispute
docs.text: The legal and policy dispute between Anthropic and BIS turns on which theory of harm justifies the licence requirement. Both positions are presented neutrally below; the BIS factual assessment has not been released.

docs.title: Administration Position: Capability-Based Theory
docs.text:
BIS's asserted position — inferred from the directive and attributed statements — is that the capability profile of Fable 5 and Mythos 5 represents an absolute risk to adversary programmes, independently of the incremental marginal capability it provides above already-accessible models. Under this theory, the question is not whether Fable 5 is meaningfully more capable than what adversaries can obtain through open-weight models or domestic AI development, but whether access to Fable 5 at all provides material assistance to programmes of concern: autonomous weapons decision-support, mass surveillance infrastructure, automated disinformation at scale, or AI-assisted weapons design.

The administration further contends — based on intelligence assessments not made public — that adversary actors sought to exploit the models in the three-day window between launch (9 June) and the directive (12 June). [Contested — see Contested Items]

docs.title: Anthropic's Position: Incremental-Risk Theory
docs.text:
Anthropic's public statement contests the licence requirement on the ground that Fable 5 and Mythos 5 do not provide meaningful incremental capability above what adversaries can already access through open-weight frontier models, jailbroken commercial models, or domestic AI programmes — notably China's. On Anthropic's account, the marginal uplift from Fable 5 / Mythos 5 access above the existing baseline is insufficient to justify the compliance cost: global access suspension, indeterminate licence adjudication delay, and material commercial and operational harm to Anthropic's customers worldwide. Anthropic does not contest BIS's authority under ECRA to impose licence requirements but disputes the factual predicate for applying that authority in this instance.

Note: Both positions are reproduced as stated by the respective parties. This entry takes no view on the merits. The BIS factual assessment and intelligence basis underlying the directive have not been publicly released.

docs.title: Contested and Unverified Items
docs.text:
The following elements of this entry carry materially higher uncertainty and should be verified independently before reliance.

Item Status Basis for uncertainty Full text of the BIS "is informed" letter Unpublished No public release; existence and basic terms inferred from Anthropic statement and practitioner analysis Specific regulatory basis (ECRA § 4817 / EAR § 744.22) Inferred Identified by commentators based on the directive's apparent theory; BIS has not publicly confirmed the statutory citation Four licence triggers Inferred Reconstructed from Anthropic's statement and EAR doctrine; precise language of the letter not confirmed Claims of competitor involvement in triggering BIS review Single-sourced / contested Reported in one outlet; not confirmed by Anthropic or BIS; denied by named competitor [check source for current status] Claims that foreign-state actors accessed models between 9–12 June Single-sourced / contested Based on intelligence claims attributed to unnamed officials in one outlet; not independently corroborated UK AISI jailbreak-progress assessment as a contributory factor Single-sourced Reported as informing BIS's assessment; not confirmed by UK AISI or BIS Pentagon "supply chain risk" designation Under litigation / contested Separately reported designation disputed by Anthropic; litigation status as of indexing unresolved

docs.title: Compliance Takeaways
docs.text: The directive illustrates a category of supply-chain risk — model-access suspension imposed by export-control directive with no advance notice — that few AI-dependent enterprises had anticipated or planned for.

docs.title: Model Dependency as a Supply-Chain / Continuity Risk
docs.text: Where a product, service, or internal workflow depends on a single external AI API, a BIS licence requirement directed at that provider can function as an unannounced force-majeure event, suspending access instantaneously and for an indeterminate period while a licence application is adjudicated. BIS adjudication timelines are not fixed by statute; in practice, applications for sensitive-technology items can remain pending for months. Enterprises should treat frontier AI API dependencies with the same supply-chain resilience discipline applied to single-source hardware components or critical cloud services.

docs.title: Suggested Contract Provisions
docs.text:
The following clause categories are directly implicated by the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 fact pattern. Enterprises procuring AI API services or embedding frontier model capabilities in products should review their agreements for the following:

Clause type Risk addressed Regulatory-suspension / force-majeure Allocates risk when an API becomes unavailable due to a government directive; defines notice obligations, price-adjustment rights, and SLA suspension Maximum-notice kill-switch Requires the provider to give the maximum feasible advance notice of imminent suspension and to cooperate in orderly transition Tested fallback model Requires the provider to maintain and test a designated fallback model available for automatic cutover on primary model suspension Portability and exit rights Preserves the customer's right to migrate inference workloads, fine-tuned models, and stored outputs to an alternative provider without penalty on regulatory suspension Multi-model / redundancy architecture Technical and contractual obligation to maintain at least one alternative inference pathway at all times; tested quarterly Export-control representations Provider represents ongoing EAR compliance; customer represents it will not use the API in breach of EAR end-use / end-user restrictions; mutual obligation to notify on receipt of BIS correspondence

Cross-reference: BIS Affiliates Rule (ownership-chain diligence that may apply to AI supply-chain counterparties); AI & Advanced Technology Export Controls (AI Diffusion Rule model-weight and capability-threshold framework).

docs.title: Sources
docs.text: Sources are tiered by reliability and proximity to the primary facts. First-party and party-to-the-dispute sources are labelled accordingly; their statements should be read in that context.

docs.title: Primary / First-Party
docs.text: Anthropic — Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Access Update (first-party — party to dispute) https://anthropic.com/news/fable-mythos-access Anthropic's public statement confirming receipt of the BIS directive, the decision to suspend global access, and the company's incremental-risk argument. The primary public source for the existence and basic terms of the directive. Readers should note this reflects Anthropic's own characterisation of the facts.

docs.title: Technical and Industry Analysis
docs.text: Cloud Security Alliance — AI Model Export Controls: SaaS Deemed-Export Risk (technical / industry body) https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org CSA whitepaper analysing the extension of deemed-export doctrine to SaaS AI inference interfaces, API access control obligations, and enterprise compliance implications. Useful for the technical framing of "release" via inference-time interaction and for the practical compliance programme recommendations.

docs.title: Established Reporting
docs.text:
Just Security Coverage of the BIS action's legal basis, the deemed-export doctrine's application to AI inference, and the broader implications for AI export controls under ECRA. Reporting based on multiple sourced contacts.

Tech Policy Press Analysis of the incremental-risk versus capability-based debate, the policy context within the AI Diffusion Rule framework, and the potential scope of the "is informed" letter mechanism if generalised to other frontier models.

Fortune Commercial and market-impact coverage of the global access suspension, Anthropic's customer communications, and competitor responses.

Computerworld / CIO Enterprise IT and compliance framing; coverage of AI API dependency risk, business continuity planning, and alternative model sourcing strategies.

docs.title: Legal and Export Practitioner Analysis
docs.text:
Volkov Law Group (export practitioner) Export-control compliance analysis of the "is informed" letter mechanism under the EAR, the deemed-export theory as applied to SaaS AI, and recommended compliance programme adjustments for AI API customers and providers.

Export Practitioner (exportprac.com) (export practitioner) Practitioner-level analysis of the four licence triggers, the EAR § 744.22 and ECRA § 4817 basis, and the interaction of this directive with the paused AI Diffusion Rule framework.

docs.title: Australia DFAT — Iran Autonomous Sanctions (2026)
docs.text:
Australian autonomous sanctions targeting Iran's campaign of oppression and destabilisation, announced by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) under Minister for Foreign Affairs Senator the Hon Penny Wong. This action aligns with contemporaneous EU measures on Iran (EU-2026-002 — HR alignment statement, May 2026) as part of a coordinated international pressure campaign.

Source status: Both source URLs (DFAT media release and DFAT Consolidated List) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. Entry is constructed from prompt-supplied metadata (title, minister, and thematic characterisation). Target identities, counts, and the specific legislative instruments used require verification at the source and via the DFAT consolidated sanctions list.

docs.title: Entry AU-2026-001 — Autonomous Sanctions Targeting Iran's Campaign of Oppression and Destabilisation
docs.text:
• Title: Autonomous sanctions targeting Iran's campaign of oppression and destabilisation
  • Date: [2026 — exact date unverified; verify at the DFAT media release]
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: Australia — Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT); Minister for Foreign Affairs, Senator the Hon Penny Wong
  • Legal instrument / basis: Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011 (Cth); Autonomous Sanctions Regulations 2011 (Cth) — the designations would be made by legislative instrument (typically an amendment to the Autonomous Sanctions (Designated Persons and Entities and Declared Countries — Iran) Regulation or a new instrument under the same Act); Consolidated Iranian Sanctions Regime
  • Targets: Multiple individuals and/or entities (exact count unverified — verify at DFAT consolidated list)
  • Sector / theme: Iran — domestic oppression; regional destabilisation; Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and affiliated entities; proxy and militia networks (Hizballah, Houthi/Ansar Allah, Iraqi militias); human rights violations; weapons proliferation
  • Summary: The Australian Government imposed autonomous sanctions on individuals and/or entities responsible for or associated with Iran's campaign of internal repression of its population and Iran's external destabilisation of regional partners and neighbours. The "campaign of oppression" language typically captures the apparatus of domestic repression — IRGC, Basij forces, judiciary officials, and media/disinformation actors involved in suppressing protests. The "destabilisation" language targets the external dimension — arms supply chains to Houthi forces in Yemen, Hizballah in Lebanon, Iraqi Popular Mobilisation Units, and former Syrian regime forces. Australia's autonomous sanctions designations in this area are administered by DFAT and implemented by Australian entities through the autonomous sanctions framework, which operates independently of but is designed to be consistent with UN Security Council sanctions.

docs.title: Coordinated Alignment with EU Iran Measures
docs.text:
This action is contemporaneous with the EU High Representative's statement (EU-2026-002, 11 May 2026) confirming that certain third countries are aligning with EU Iran restrictive measures. While Australia's autonomous sanctions are formally independent, the policy context is the same: concerted multilateral pressure on Iran across the US, EU, UK, Australia, and aligned partner states. Compliance practitioners monitoring Iran exposure should treat this as a multi-jurisdiction tightening signal.

Jurisdiction Action in this wave Basis EU HR alignment statement (EU-2026-002) EU Iran sanctions Regulations 359/2011, 267/2012 Australia Autonomous sanctions (AU-2026-001) Autonomous Sanctions Act 2011 (Cth) US (OFAC) Ongoing "Economic Fury" Iran actions (see digest) IEEPA / IFSR UK (OFSI) Ongoing Iran financial sanctions (monitoring) SAMLA 2018

docs.title: EU Sanctions — May 2026 Wave (11 May 2026)
docs.text:
Three European Union actions published on 11 May 2026: a human-rights designation targeting the forced transfer of Ukrainian children to Russia; a High Representative statement on third-country alignment with EU Iran restrictive measures; and the Council's restoration of the full EU–Syria Cooperation Agreement. These three actions on a single date reflect the EU's simultaneous use of sanctions as a tool of both tightening (Ukraine/Russia, Iran) and normalisation (Syria).

Source status: All three source URLs returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. Entries are built from prompt-supplied metadata (which specifies target counts and action titles) and the EU's published legal framework. Named designated parties and the specific Council Decision/Regulation numbers require verification at consilium.europa.eu.

docs.title: Entry EU-2026-001 — Forced Transfer of Ukrainian Children (11 May 2026)
docs.text:
• Title: Ukrainian children unlawfully deported and forcibly transferred to Russia — EU sanctions 16 individuals and seven entities
  • Date: 2026-05-11
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: EU — Council of the European Union
  • Legal instrument / basis: EU Russia-related restrictive measures framework — Council Regulation (EU) No 269/2014 (as amended) concerning actions undermining Ukraine's territorial integrity; and/or EU Global Human Rights Sanctions Regime — Council Regulation (EU) 2020/1998. The specific legal vehicle (which Regulation or Decision) requires source verification.
  • Targets: 16 individuals and 7 entities designated
  • Sector / theme: Forced deportation; forced transfer of Ukrainian children; international humanitarian law violations; human rights; war crimes
  • Summary: The EU Council designated 16 individuals and 7 entities responsible for the unlawful deportation and forcible transfer of Ukrainian children from Russian-occupied Ukrainian territory to Russia. The International Criminal Court issued arrest warrants in March 2023 for President Putin and Russian Children's Rights Commissioner Maria Lvova-Belova in connection with this same conduct; EU designation extends accountability through the asset freeze and travel ban mechanism to a broader range of responsible officials and facilitating entities. Designation prohibits EU persons from making funds or economic resources available to the named parties and requires asset freezes by EU credit and financial institutions.
  • Cross-references: US-2026-003 (OFAC action on same date — thematic link plausible, unconfirmed); ICC arrest warrants (March 2023); OFAC SDN & Sanctions List Search
  • Source URL: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/11/ukrainian-children-unlawfully-deported-and-forcibly-transferred-to-russia-eu-sanctions-16-individuals-and-seven-entities/

docs.title: Entry EU-2026-002 — HR Statement: Third-Country Alignment on Iran Restrictive Measures (11 May 2026)
docs.text:
• Title: Statement by the High Representative on behalf of the EU on the alignment of certain countries concerning restrictive measures directed against certain persons and entities in view of the situation in Iran
  • Date: 2026-05-11
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: EU — High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy (speaking on behalf of the EU)
  • Legal instrument / basis: EU restrictive measures on Iran — Council Regulation (EU) No 359/2011 (human rights violations); Council Regulation (EU) No 267/2012 (non-proliferation / nuclear programme); related Council Decisions. Third-country alignment operates through political declarations by partner states adopting equivalent measures at the national level.
  • Targets: This is an alignment statement — not itself a designation action. The named third countries confirm they are aligning their own national restrictive measures with existing EU Iran sanctions lists. Countries that routinely align include EU candidate states (Western Balkans: North Macedonia, Montenegro, Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Kosovo), Eastern Partnership members (Moldova, Georgia), and others.
  • Sector / theme: Iran — human rights (domestic oppression); nuclear non-proliferation; regional destabilisation; third-country multilateral alignment
  • Summary: The EU High Representative confirmed that a group of partner countries have aligned their national measures with EU restrictive measures directed against persons and entities connected to the situation in Iran. Alignment statements extend the effective geographic reach of EU sanctions beyond EU member states without requiring the aligned countries to enact independent legal frameworks. This statement is contemporaneous with Australia's DFAT autonomous sanctions targeting Iran's campaign of oppression and destabilisation (AU-2026-001) — together these constitute a coordinated multi-jurisdiction Iran pressure campaign in May 2026. Compliance practitioners should note that the same individuals/entities on EU Iran lists will now also be caught by the national measures of the aligned countries, increasing the extraterritorial compliance burden for multinationals.
  • Cross-references: AU-2026-001 (Australia DFAT Iran autonomous sanctions — coordinated alignment); OFAC IFSR Iran programme
  • Source URL: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/11/statement-by-the-high-representative-on-behalf-of-the-eu-on-the-alignment-of-certain-countries-concerning-restrictive-measures-directed-against-certain-persons-and-entities-in-view-of-the-situation-in-iran/
  • Retrieved: 2026-06-16 — HTTP 403 ⚠

docs.title: Entry EU-2026-003 — Syria: Restoration of Full Application of EU–Syria Cooperation Agreement (11 May 2026)
docs.text:
• Title: Syria — Council restores full application of EU–Syria Cooperation Agreement
  • Date: 2026-05-11
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: EU — Council of the European Union
  • Legal instrument / basis: EU–Syria Association/Cooperation Agreement; Council Decision and/or Regulation suspending or reinstating the agreement. Context: EU Syria sanctions under Council Regulation (EU) No 36/2012 (as amended through successive restrictive measures packages targeting the Assad regime).
  • Targets: N/A — this is a sanctions-easing / normalisation decision, not a new designation action.
  • Sector / theme: Syria — post-conflict normalisation; sanctions relief; re-engagement compliance; contrast with simultaneous tightening in this wave
  • Summary: The EU Council restored full application of the EU–Syria Cooperation Agreement, a significant step in normalising economic and political engagement with post-Assad Syria. Following the fall of the Assad regime in December 2024, the EU began a phased approach to easing Syria sanctions: initially suspending specific sectoral restrictions to enable humanitarian access and reconstruction, and progressively lifting suspensions as the new Syrian authorities consolidate governance. Restoring the Cooperation Agreement unlocks trade preferences, development financing, and institutional engagement. Compliance note for EU persons and entities re-engaging with Syria: While this decision signals normalisation, residual Syria sanctions (designations of Assad-era officials and entities, arms embargo provisions) likely remain in force — legal clearance is required before resuming previously prohibited activities. A bespoke sanctions review is advisable before any Syrian counterparty is onboarded.

docs.title: Easing vs. Tightening — Contrast with Simultaneous Actions
docs.text:
On the same date (11 May 2026), the EU also: - Designated 16 individuals and 7 entities for the forced transfer of Ukrainian children (EU-2026-001) — tightening - Issued an Iran alignment statement (EU-2026-002) — tightening / multilateral pressure

The Syria decision demonstrates that the EU uses its sanctions toolkit flexibly — easing where normalisation is the policy goal while simultaneously tightening against Russia and Iran. For compliance teams, this means Syria review processes must distinguish between what remains prohibited and what has been unlocked.

  • Cross-references: EU-2026-001 and EU-2026-002 (simultaneous tightening actions on same date); EU Syria sanctions regime (Council Regulation (EU) No 36/2012)
  • Source URL: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/11/syria-council-restores-full-application-of-eu-syria-cooperation-agreement/
  • Retrieved: 2026-06-16 — HTTP 403 ⚠

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.text:
• Title: UAE designates individuals and five entities on its local list
  • Date: [2026-06 — exact date unverified; verify at source]
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: UAE — National Anti-Money Laundering and Combatting the Financing of Terrorism and Financing of Illegal Organisations Committee (NAMLCFTC), implemented through the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE)
  • Legal instrument / basis: UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 20 of 2018 on Anti-Money Laundering and Combatting the Financing of Terrorism and Financing of Illegal Organisations (as amended); Cabinet Decision No. 74 of 2020 on the Terrorism Lists Regulation and its Implementing Regulation; Federal Law No. 7 of 2014 on Combatting Terrorism (as amended)

The UAE has been an active FATF grey-list country working towards removal by demonstrating effective AML/CFT frameworks; designations such as this one form part of that demonstrated effectiveness. Asset-freeze and prohibition obligations arise immediately upon designation for UAE-nexus transactions and relationships.

docs.title: Correspondent Banking and Screening Relevance
docs.text:
Financial institutions with UAE-correspondent or UAE-counterparty exposure should:

  1. Maintain a separate screening universe for the UAE local list (CBUAE portal) in addition to UN/OFAC/EU/UK lists.
  2. Check whether any of the designated individuals or entities overlap with persons already on other major lists (potential cross-designation — deduplication required).
  3. Note that UAE-dirham-denominated transactions and transactions routed through UAE correspondent banks may be subject to UAE local list compliance obligations, even where the transaction is not otherwise UAE-regulated.

docs.text:
• UAE is a major hub for commodity trading, shipping, and re-export activity; local list designees may appear in shipping, trade finance, or commodity chains.
  • Any company with UAE operations (offices, subsidiaries, bank accounts) faces direct obligations under UAE law.

  • Correspondent banks maintaining nostro/vostro relationships with UAE banks should ensure their UAE-side due diligence covers the local list.

docs.title: Entry UK-2026-003 — "Largest Ever Shadow Fleet" PM Announcement
docs.text:
• Title: Prime Minister to announce largest ever sanctions package targeting shadow fleet as UK ramps up pressure on Russia
  • Date: ~2026-02 (likely February 2026, around the fourth anniversary of the full-scale invasion; alternatively June 2026 — verify at source)
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: UK — Prime Minister's Office / FCDO / OFSI
  • Legal instrument / basis: Russia (Sanctions) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019; SAMLA 2018
  • Targets: Shadow fleet vessels (tankers and support ships enabling Russian oil exports in excess of the G7 price cap); associated shipowners, managers, and insurers (exact vessel count requires source verification)
  • Sector / theme: Russia sanctions — shadow fleet; oil price cap enforcement; maritime/tanker sector; G7 coordination
  • Summary: The Prime Minister's announcement characterised this as the UK's "largest ever" shadow fleet-targeting package. Shadow fleet vessels are typically third-country-flagged tankers carrying Russian crude above the USD 60/barrel G7 price cap without access to Western services (insurance, P&I clubs, ship finance, crewing). UK designation of individual vessels prevents UK-nexus operators from providing maritime services to them. The package signals continued UK commitment to the G7 price cap coalition and represents an escalation in direct vessel targeting. See Russian Oil Price Cap for the compliance framework applicable to tanker operators.
  • Cross-references: UK-2026-001 (formal designations list); UK-2026-004 (anniversary package context); Russian Oil Price Cap
  • Source URL: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/prime-minister-to-announce-largest-ever-sanctions-package-targeting-shadow-fleet-as-uk-ramps-up-pressure-on-russia

docs.text:
• Title: UK announces biggest sanctions package against Russia, four years on from full-scale invasion of Ukraine
  • Date: ~2026-02-24 (fourth anniversary of the full-scale invasion; verify exact publication date at source)
  • Jurisdiction / Authority: UK — FCDO / OFSI
  • Legal instrument / basis: Russia (Sanctions) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019; SAMLA 2018
  • Targets: Multiple individuals, entities, and vessels (described as the "biggest" Russia package by target count at that point — exact numbers require source verification)
  • Sector / theme: Russia sanctions — comprehensive anniversary package; shadow fleet; defence-industrial base; financial enablers; oligarchs
  • Summary: On or around the fourth anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (24 February 2022), the UK Government announced what it described as its biggest Russia sanctions package to date. Anniversary packages typically target a broad range of actors: defence-industrial supply chains, financial facilitators, persons connected to the Kremlin, and shadow fleet operators. The "four years on" framing positions the UK's sanctions effort as a sustained, long-term commitment rather than a reactive response. The formal designations implementing this package may be reflected in the June 2026 designations list (UK-2026-001).
  • Cross-references: UK-2026-001 (formal designations list); UK-2026-003 (shadow fleet announcement); Russian Oil Price Cap
  • Source URL: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/uk-announces-biggest-sanctions-package-against-russia-four-years-on-from-full-scale-invasion-of-ukraine

docs.title: AI & Advanced Technology Export Controls
docs.text: Analysis and commentary on BIS controls targeting advanced computing chips, AI model weights, and related software. The regulatory landscape has evolved rapidly since the October 2022 advanced-computing rules; the sources below cover the January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule and subsequent developments.

docs.title: Sidley Austin — New US Export Controls on Advanced Computing & AI Model Weights (Jan 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.sidley.com/en/insights/newsupdates/2025/01/new-us-export-controls-on-advanced-computing-items-and-artificial-intelligence-model-weights

Comprehensive overview of the January 2025 Interim Final Rule. Covers the three-tier country framework, new ECCN classifications for AI model weights, the role of Data Center Validated End Users (DCVEUs), and implications for cloud providers and hyperscalers. Includes a comparison to the October 2022 and October 2023 rules.

docs.title: Baker McKenzie Sanctions News — BIS Interim Final Rule on AI & Advanced Computing ICs
docs.text:
URL: https://sanctionsnews.bakermckenzie.com/bis-issues-interim-final-rule-and-call-for-comments-on-new-export-controls-on-artificial-intelligence-and-advanced-computing-integrated-circuits/

Focus on the integrated-circuit controls component of the AI Diffusion Rule, including new ECCN 3A090 parameters and the Total Processing Performance (TPP) thresholds that determine licensing requirements. Useful for hardware manufacturers and chip designers.

docs.title: WilmerHale — BIS Issues Long-Awaited Export Controls on AI (Feb 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/publications/20250205-bis-issues-long-awaited-export-controls-on-ai

Analysis of the policy rationale behind the AI Diffusion Rule and its interaction with existing EAR controls. Addresses the model-weight definition, the "publicly available" exclusion, and compliance programme expectations for AI developers.

docs.title: National Law Review — BIS Issues Four Key Updates: Advanced Computing & AI Export Controls
docs.text:
URL: https://natlawreview.com/article/bis-issues-four-key-updates-advanced-computing-and-ai-export-controls

Summary of four simultaneous regulatory updates accompanying the Diffusion Rule: (1) Entity List additions, (2) Military End-User (MEU) list expansion, (3) Validated End-User (VEU) programme changes, and (4) new Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR) provisions targeting AI supply chains.

docs.title: Mayer Brown — Commerce Announces New Export Compliance Expectations for AI (May 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/05/us-commerce-department-announces-new-export-compliance-expectations-related-to-artificial-intelligence

docs.title: SCALE Act — AI Chip Export Parity Controls (April 2026)
docs.text:
Source: Export Compliance Daily, 22 April 2026; H.R. introduced by Rep. John Moolenaar (R-Mich.), Chairman, House Select Committee on China

The Semiconductor Controls Adjusted to Limit Exports (SCALE) Act would require the Commerce Department to set annual limits on exports of advanced AI chips to entities in China and other countries of concern, calibrated to prevent adversaries from achieving parity with US AI hardware capacity.

Key provisions:

  • Parity threshold: Exports permitted only up to 110% of the performance of chips adversaries can independently produce in significant quantities — permitting continued US competitiveness without enabling capability leapfrogging.
  • 5% hardware-base cap: License applications subject to a presumption of denial if exports would cause adversary-controlled AI hardware to exceed 5% of total US AI hardware capacity.
  • Annual assessment: Annual evaluation of adversary chipmaking capability sets dynamic export thresholds.
  • Supply-chain certification: Applicants must certify that exports would not disrupt US supply chains or domestic demand.

The SCALE Act was referred to the House Foreign Affairs and Intelligence committees.

docs.title: Match Act — Allied Alignment on Semiconductor Equipment Export Controls (June 2026)
docs.text:
Source: Export Compliance Daily, 15 June 2026; AI Policy Network coalition letter to Sens. Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) and Andy Kim (D-N.J.), 11 June 2026

A 14-member coalition led by the AI Policy Network endorsed the Match Act (introduced April 2026, Ricketts/Kim), which would require US allies to match US export restrictions on semiconductor manufacturing equipment sales to China.

Coalition argument: "American semiconductor equipment firms now comply with the strictest export controls in the world. However, our allies have not kept pace with these controls, putting our companies at a commercial disadvantage while accelerating China's capabilities."

Legislative status:

Chamber Action Date House Foreign Affairs Committee approved, 36–8 April 2026 Senate Banking Committee markup urged by Ricketts, Kim, and Warren June 2026 AI hearing

Notable co-signers include Saif Khan (former NSC Director for Technology & National Security), Chris McGuire (former NSC Deputy Senior Director), Nicholas Brown (former BIS Senior Adviser), Samuel Hammond (Foundation for American Innovation), and Eric Gastfriend (Americans for Responsible Innovation).

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: BIS Affiliates Rule & 50% Ownership Expansion
docs.text:
⚠ Snap-back date: 10 November 2026 — currently suspended (Phase 1)

The BIS Affiliates Rule expansion is suspended for one year (10 Nov 2025 – 9 Nov 2026) under a US-China trade deal framework (FR 2025-19846, 90 FR 47201). The expansion will automatically reimpose on 10 Nov 2026 (Phase 2) unless BIS issues a further extension or repeal before that date. The baseline EAR Entity List and MEU List controls remain fully in effect throughout the suspension window. Compliance programmes should complete affiliate-mapping and diligence exercises before the Phase 2 date.

The snap-back countdown and latest status updates are maintained automatically by the bis-affiliates-monitor.yml GitHub Actions workflow.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The BIS Affiliates Rule mirrors OFAC's 50% Rule in the export-control context: it treats affiliates of Entity List or MEU List parties as themselves subject to licence requirements, even if the affiliate is not independently listed. This closes a significant compliance gap where controlled-party transactions were routed through unlisted subsidiaries or joint-venture vehicles.

The rule applies to: - Entities owned 50% or more by a single Entity List / MEU List party - Entities owned 50% or more in the aggregate by two or more such parties - Both direct and indirect ownership chains

docs.title: Encryption Controls, Open Source & EAR Scope
docs.text: Encryption items have their own licensing and reporting framework under EAR Part 742.15 and Supplement No. 3. Many AI and software products incorporate encryption, making these rules directly relevant. Open-source software raises distinct questions about EAR applicability.

docs.text:

docs.title: Extraterritorial Jurisdiction & Enforcement
docs.text: US export controls and sanctions have extensive extraterritorial reach through mechanisms like the Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR), the de minimis rule, and correspondent-banking pressure. This page collects resources on how BIS and OFAC enforce controls beyond US borders.

Covers extraterritorial reach over non-US chip fabs, the "substantial transformation" test, and practical compliance steps for foreign chip manufacturers and distributors. docs.title: OFAC UBO Aggregation & 50% Rule
docs.text:
OFAC's 50% Rule is one of the most consequential and least well-understood rules in US sanctions compliance. It extends SDN-equivalent restrictions to any entity owned 50% or more (individually or in aggregate) by one or more SDN-listed parties — even if that entity does not itself appear on the SDN List.

See also: Felipe Villasuso Lago, "The Affiliate Trap: OFAC's 50% Rule and Aggregation Risk in Joint Ventures", Oxford Business Law Blog (OBLB), 2026. The article analyses how the aggregation mechanic creates hidden sanctions exposure in multi-party joint ventures and private equity structures, with particular focus on the oil & gas sector.

docs.title: OFAC FAQ — Topic 1521: The 50% Rule
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/faqs/topic/1521

The authoritative OFAC guidance on the 50% Rule. Explains that US persons are prohibited from dealing with entities owned 50% or more by one or more SDN-designated parties, even absent a separate SDN designation of the entity. Addresses aggregation across multiple SDN owners, indirect ownership chains, and the interaction with sectoral sanctions.

Key points from the FAQ: - Ownership is assessed at the time of the transaction - Aggregation applies even where no single SDN owns 50%+ alone - The rule applies to all OFAC sanctions programmes unless a specific programme states otherwise - OFAC does not maintain a comprehensive list of blocked entities under the 50% Rule — parties must conduct their own due diligence

docs.title: Sanctionfy — OFAC 50% Rule (2026 Commentary)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.sanctionfy.com/blog/2026/ofac-50-percent-rule

Current practitioner commentary on OFAC's 50% Rule, covering recent enforcement actions that turned on UBO aggregation issues, evolving best-practice approaches for beneficial ownership screening, and the interaction of the 50% Rule with FinCEN's Corporate Transparency Act (CTA) beneficial ownership database.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: PRC Entities & Critical Minerals — Africa (H.R. 9087)
docs.text: Proposed US legislation requiring annual public disclosure of Chinese entities carrying out critical-minerals mining in Africa under conditions of forced labour or environmental harm. The amendment to H.R. 9087, introduced 8 June 2026 by Rep. Miller of Ohio, reflects Congressional attention to China's role in African resource extraction and its intersection with supply-chain enforcement, sanctions, and export-control policy.

docs.title: H.R. 9087 Amendment — Annual List of PRC Mining Entities in Africa (June 2026)
docs.text:
Source: Amendment in the Nature of a Substitute to H.R. 9087, offered by Rep. Miller of Ohio, 8 June 2026

The amendment directs the Secretary of State to publish — at least annually for five years from enactment — a list of:

  1. Each PRC entity reasonably assessed to be carrying out mining (large-scale or artisanal/small-scale) of critical minerals, gold, or iron in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Nigeria, Guinea, Zambia, South Africa, Zimbabwe, or other African countries either:
  2. (A) using forced labour (section 307, Tariff Act of 1930, 19 U.S.C. 1307); or

  3. (B) causing environmental harm to a protected area (water contamination, soil degradation, or ecosystem/biodiversity loss).

  4. Each mine, mining zone, or concession at which such mining occurs.

The list must be publicly available, including online, in unclassified form. The version submitted to the relevant congressional committees (Senate Foreign Relations; House Foreign Affairs) may include a classified annex.

docs.title: Definition of "PRC Entity" — Broad Affiliate Scope
docs.text:
Category Covered Government/Party control Entities under ownership, control, or influence of the PRC Government, CCP, or PRC military/intelligence Organised under PRC law Entities subject to PRC jurisdiction, including Hong Kong and Macau Majority-owned/controlled Entities majority-owned, controlled, or financed by any PRC entity Affiliates & JVs Parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, contractors, and joint ventures in which a PRC entity holds a controlling interest

This broad definition mirrors the affiliate-aggregation logic of the BIS Affiliates Rule (see BIS Affiliates Rule) and OFAC's 50% Rule (see OFAC UBO & 50% Rule).

docs.title: Other Key Definitions
docs.text: Term Definition Critical mineral Section 7002(a), Energy Act of 2020 (30 U.S.C. 1606(a)) Forced labour Section 307, Tariff Act of 1930 (19 U.S.C. 1307) — the same reference used in the Uyghur Forced Labour Prevention Act Artisanal & small-scale mining Rudimentary, labour-intensive, frequently unregulated mining with inhumane working conditions Large-scale mining Capital-intensive, mechanised, industrial-scale operations by large commercial entities Protected area National parks, game refuges, ecosystem reserves, or other nature preserves in the country concerned Environmental harm Contamination of water resources (streams, rivers, lakes, wells, aquifers, soil); soil degradation or erosion; degradation of aquatic/terrestrial ecosystems or biodiversity loss

docs.title: List Development Requirements
docs.text: The Secretary of State must: - Draw on open-source information — press sources, academic, non-profit, and non-state research organisations, plus information from US embassies. - Consult with the Secretaries of Labor (Bureau of International Labor Affairs), Commerce, and Treasury; the Director of National Intelligence; other Federal agency heads; and foreign-government counterparts in the listed countries.

docs.title: Compliance Relevance
docs.text:
• Supply-chain due diligence: Companies sourcing critical minerals from African operations should assess whether upstream suppliers qualify as PRC entities under this definition, including via JV structures and affiliate chains.
  • Precursor risk: Inclusion on the list could precede import restrictions (paralleling the Uyghur Forced Labour Prevention Act's rebuttable-presumption mechanism), supply-chain sanctions, or heightened lender/investor scrutiny.
  • Forced-labour cross-reference: The Tariff Act section 307 definition underpins existing CBP enforcement mechanisms; a finding under this amendment could reinforce parallel CBP action on affected mineral imports.
  • JV governance: The express inclusion of controlling-interest JVs signals that minority positions held through a PRC-controlled JV partner will be scrutinised for affiliate-scope purposes.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Primary Regulations & Official Sources
docs.text: Authoritative primary sources for US export controls and sanctions. These are the starting point for any compliance analysis.

docs.title: Export Administration Regulations (EAR) — Full Text
docs.text:
URL: https://media.bis.gov/regulations/ear

The complete codified text of the EAR (15 C.F.R. Parts 730–774), administered by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), US Department of Commerce. The EAR controls exports, re-exports, and in-country transfers of dual-use goods, software, and technology. Key parts include Part 734 (scope), Part 738 (CCL), Part 742 (controls for specific reasons), Part 744 (end-user/end-use controls), and Part 774 (Commerce Control List with ECCNs).

docs.title: AI Diffusion Rule — Federal Register
docs.text:
URL: https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/01/15/2025-00636/framework-for-artificial-intelligence-diffusion

The January 2025 Interim Final Rule establishing a tiered framework for exports of advanced AI model weights and associated computing infrastructure. Creates three tiers of countries with differentiated licensing requirements. Note: implementation has been paused pending further rulemaking — see Extraterritorial Enforcement.

docs.title: OFAC Homepage
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov

The Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), US Department of the Treasury. Administers and enforces economic and trade sanctions based on US foreign policy and national security goals against targeted foreign countries, regimes, terrorists, proliferators, narcotics traffickers, and other threats. Primary portal for SDN list searches, programme information, licences, and FAQs.

docs.title: OFAC Sanctions Programs & Country Information
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/sanctions-programs-and-country-information

Consolidated index of all active OFAC sanctions programmes, including comprehensive (country-based) and list-based (targeted) programmes. Relevant energy-sector programmes include Russia-related (RUSAGG), Iran (IFSR), Venezuela (JCGC), and Sudan.

docs.title: DOJ National Security Division — Export Controls & Sanctions
docs.text:
URL: https://www.justice.gov/nsd/export-control-and-sanctions

The Department of Justice, National Security Division (NSD) coordinates federal criminal enforcement of export control and sanctions laws. Resources include enforcement priority statements, charging guidance, and major case summaries. Criminal penalties under the Export Control Reform Act (ECRA) and the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) are enforced here.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Sanctions Programs, Enforcement & Oil/Energy Sector
docs.text: The oil, gas, and broader energy sector sits at the intersection of many of the most significant US sanctions programmes. Russia, Iran, Venezuela, and Sudan are the primary exposure jurisdictions; the Russia-related programmes have expanded most rapidly since 2022 and now include sector-specific and price-cap measures that affect tanker operators, trading firms, insurers, and financiers globally.

docs.title: OFAC — Industry Groups Information
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/additional-ofac-resources/ofac-information-for-industry-groups

OFAC's dedicated guidance portal for specific industry sectors, including energy and petroleum, financial services, maritime shipping, and insurance. Contains sector-specific FAQs, guidance documents, and links to relevant general licences. The energy-sector page addresses the Russian oil price cap mechanism and related compliance expectations for operators in the shipping and financing chain.

docs.title: OFAC Press Releases
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/press-releases

Official press releases for all OFAC designations, general licence issuances, enforcement actions, and guidance updates. Subscribe to the RSS feed or monitor this page for real-time sanctions programme changes. Energy-sector relevant releases include Russia energy-related designations, Iran oil-related actions, and Venezuela sectoral updates.

docs.title: OFAC SDN & Sanctions List Search
docs.text:
URL: https://sanctionssearch.ofac.treas.gov/

The official OFAC consolidated sanctions list search tool. Searches the Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) List, Sectoral Sanctions Identifications (SSI) List, Foreign Sanctions Evaders (FSE) List, and other OFAC-maintained lists simultaneously. Essential for counterparty screening; note that the 50% Rule (see OFAC UBO & 50% Rule) means list-search alone is insufficient — UBO analysis is also required.

docs.title: Russian Oil Price Cap
docs.text:
The G7 price cap on Russian seaborne crude (USD 60/barrel) and refined products imposes compliance obligations on all service providers in the tanker supply chain — shipowners, charterers, brokers, insurers, and financial institutions — regardless of nationality. OFAC enforces the cap through its existing Russia-related programmes. Non-compliance can result in SDN designation of vessels and operators.

Last reviewed: June 2026
